content.problemStatement:
In 1970, John Conway created a cellular automaton, the Game of Life.

In this problem, we will consider a modified version of the Game of Life called VastoLorde's Game of Life, and we will be focusing on just one single second of change.

Suppose you start with grid AAA which has dimensions H×WH \times WH×W. The top left cell being A[1][1]A[1][1]A[1][1] and the bottom right cell being A[H][W]A[H][W]A[H][W]. Each cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) of this grid is either "dead" or "alive". An alive cell is represented by 1 and a dead cell by 0.

When Vasto clicks his fingers, this grid changes into a different grid, BBB. BBB's dimensions are (H−1)×(W−1)(H-1) \times (W-1)(H−1)×(W−1). The state of the (i,j)(i,j)(i,j)-th cell of BBB is determined by the following rule:

Consider cells A[i][j]A[i][j]A[i][j], A[i+1][j]A[i+1][j]A[i+1][j], A[i][j+1]A[i][j+1]A[i][j+1] and A[i+1][j+1]A[i+1][j+1]A[i+1][j+1] i.e. the 2x2 block with (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) at the top left corner. If exactly one diagonal of this 2x2 block is alive and the other two cells are dead, then the cell B[i][j]B[i][j]B[i][j] will be alive. Otherwise, B[i][j]B[i][j]B[i][j] will be dead.

Formally, if either (A[i][j]=1A[i][j] = 1A[i][j]=1, A[i+1][j+1]=1A[i+1][j+1] = 1A[i+1][j+1]=1, A[i+1][j]=0A[i+1][j] = 0A[i+1][j]=0, A[i][j+1]=0A[i][j+1] = 0A[i][j+1]=0), or (A[i][j]=0A[i][j] = 0A[i][j]=0, A[i+1][j+1]=0A[i+1][j+1] = 0A[i+1][j+1]=0, A[i+1][j]=1A[i+1][j] = 1A[i+1][j]=1, A[i][j+1]=1A[i][j+1] = 1A[i][j+1]=1), then B[i][j]=1B[i][j] = 1B[i][j]=1. If neither of these two configurations are present, then B[i][j]=0B[i][j] = 0B[i][j]=0.

For example, suppose H=3H=3H=3, W=3W=3W=3, and grid AAA is

`101  
011  
101  
`This would change into the following 2 x 2 BBB grid:

`10  
10  
`Your task is, given grid BBB, determine the number of possible grids AAA that will generate this grid when Vasto clicks his finger.

Print your answer modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer, TTT, the number of testcases.
The first line of each testcase contains two integers WWW and HHH, the width and height of BBB. HHH lines follow.
The iii-th line of each testcase contains WWW space separated integers B[i][1],B[i][2],…,B[i][W]B[i][1], B[i][2], \dots, B[i][W]B[i][1],B[i][2],…,B[i][W] such that the cell B[i][j]B[i][j]B[i][j] of the grid is alive if B[i][j]=1B[i][j]= 1B[i][j]=1 and dead if B[i][j]=0B[i][j] = 0B[i][j]=0.


Output Format
Print a single integer in a new line for each testcase, the number of grids AAA that create the given grid BBB modulo 109+710^9 + 7109+7.
content.inputFormat: The first line of input contains a single integer, T, the number of testcases. The first line of each testcase contains two integers, W and H, the width and height of BBB. H lines follow. The i-th line of each testcase contains W space-separated integers B[i][1], B[i][2], …, B[i][W] such that the cell B[i][j] of the grid is alive if B[i][j] = 1 and dead if B[i][j] = 0.
content.outputFormat: Print a single integer in a new line for each testcase, the number of grids AAA that create the given grid BBB modulo 10^9 + 7.

sampleTestCases.explanation: There are two possible grids AAA that create the given grid BBB.
There is one possible grid AAA that creates the given grid BBB.
There is one possible grid AAA that creates the given grid BBB.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use dynamic programming to count the number of possible grids AAA that create the given grid BBB. We start by initializing a 2D array dp with dimensions (H+1) x (W+1), where dp[i][j] represents the number of possible grids AAA that create the given grid BBB up to the i-th row and j-th column. We then fill up the dp array row by row, using the given rules to determine the number of possible grids AAA that create the given grid BBB.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * H * W) — we loop over each test case, and for each test case, we loop over each cell in the grid BBB.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(H * W) — we need to store the dp array, which has dimensions (H+1) x (W+1).
